summary: Uncle Bob presents a formal oath for programmers, modeled after professional oaths like the Hippocratic Oath for doctors. The oath consists of nine promises covering code quality, testing, collaboration, honesty, and continuous improvement. It frames programming as a profession with ethical obligations rather than merely a job. The oath emphasizes that programmers have a responsibility to defend the honor of their craft through disciplined practice.
key_points: Programming should be treated as a profession with formal ethical commitments, not just a technical job
Code quality is a moral obligation - programmers must never knowingly allow defective code to accumulate
Every release requires proof that the code works (implicit advocacy for TDD and automated testing)
Professional programmers have obligations to their teammates - covering for each other and not impeding progress
Honest estimation without false certainty is an ethical requirement of the profession
money_quotes: I will not produce harmful code.
I will not knowingly allow code that is defective either in behavior or structure to accumulate.
I will produce, with each release, a quick, sure, and repeatable proof that every element of the code works as it should.
I will fearlessly and relentlessly improve my creations at every opportunity. I will never degrade them.
I will do all that I can to keep the productivity of myself, and others, as high as possible.
I will produce estimates that are honest both in magnitude and precision. I will not make promises without certainty.
I will never stop learning and improving my craft.
key_insight: Programming is a profession that demands formal ethical commitments to quality, honesty, and continuous improvement - not merely technical competence.
